patterns: container-per-service: backend, frontend, worker, redis, postgres each in their own container
docker-compose-environments: separate compose files for dev, test, and production
health-checks: every service exposes a /health endpoint
twelve-factor: config in environment, logs to stdout, stateless processes
blue-green-deployment: zero-downtime deploys by running new version alongside old
load-balancing: Nginx as reverse proxy with upstream blocks for horizontal scaling
principles: Docker Compose for development and single-server production — Kubernetes only at true scale.
Every service has a health check that verifies its dependencies (DB connection, Redis ping).
Secrets are never in Docker images or git — use environment variables injected at runtime.
HTTPS everywhere — Certbot/Let's Encrypt for automated certificate management.
Logs are structured JSON to stdout — centralized logging is a layer on top, not baked in.
CI/CD runs tests, builds images, and deploys on merge to main — no manual deploys.
Monitor cost, latency, and error rates post-deployment — not just 'is it up?'

mcp_deployment.description: Deployment and environment patterns for MCP servers — local and cloud.

uv_environment.role: high-performance unified package manager — supersedes venv + pip for environment consistency
lifecycle: uv init: create consistent project foundation
uv add 'mcp[cli]': core protocol and CLI tools
uv add --dev ipykernel: development tools kept separate from production
uv run server.py: execute with managed dependencies

script_metadata.description: Embed dependencies directly in the script header for self-documenting, portable MCP servers.
script_metadata.benefit: UV resolves and installs into a temporary cache during execution — the server is fully portable across any machine with UV installed
script_metadata.rule: Every line within the metadata block must be commented with # — failure breaks execution

desktop.config: Edit Claude Desktop config.json in developer settings
desktop.requirement: absolute paths mandatory for enterprise stability — ensures correct initialization regardless of working directory

claude_code.config: Add MCP server to .claude/settings.json or user settings
claude_code.verify: claude mcp list — confirms registered servers
claude_code.advantage: LLM can execute tools AND modify local files based on output

claude_integration.security: Allow Once vs. Always Allow permission model — user controls execution, proprietary scripts run only with explicit permission

tool_chaining.description: Multiple MCP servers chain together — a single natural language prompt triggers a sequence: data extraction → processing → report generation → file storage.
tool_chaining.principle: Each MCP server is a modular capability. Compose them like Unix pipes — each does one thing well.

patterns: cache-aside: check cache first, fall back to DB, populate cache on miss
ttl-based: all cache entries have explicit TTL, no indefinite caching
cache-invalidation: invalidate on write, not on timer

load_balancing.scaling_path: vertical first (bigger VPS), horizontal when CPU consistently >70%

patterns: idempotent-tasks: every task can be safely retried
dead-letter-queue: failed tasks after N retries go to DLQ for manual inspection
priority-queues: separate queues for urgent (auth, notifications) vs. bulk (ingestion, reports)
task-timeout: every task has a max execution time

event_driven.description: Redis + Celery create a logical disconnect between API requests and processing — enables unattended 24/7 execution, absorbs traffic bursts without overwhelming the server.
use_cases: user-created: triggers welcome email and default settings
webhook-received: raw payload persisted to DB, then async dispatched to worker

steps: 1. signature-verification: verify incoming webhook against provider signatures (see security.json for details)
2. data-persistence: immediately save raw payload to PostgreSQL — leaves a trace for debugging even if downstream processing fails
3. async-dispatch: hand off to Celery worker, freeing the API to handle the next event instantly
webhook_handling.principle: Never process webhook payloads synchronously — always persist first, process async.

operational_tiers.description: Three-tiered model separating automated triggers, recurring schedules, and conversational agents. Start with Tiers 1-2 for reliability, add Tier 3 for dynamic intelligence.

tier_1_triggers.name: Trigger-Based Actions
tier_1_triggers.reliability: highest — deterministic logic, no LLM involved

tier_2_scheduled.name: Scheduled Workflows
tier_2_scheduled.advanced: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) system in Python for complex multi-step workflows — provides n8n/Zapier-style sequential logic within controlled code

tier_3_agents.name: Agent Layer
tier_3_agents.pattern: dynamic, generative decision-making based on user input
tier_3_agents.reliability: experimental — requires human-in-the-loop refinement and guardrails
tier_3_agents.principle: Agents are powerful but volatile. Gate agent actions behind confirmation for anything destructive or irreversible.

hitl_patterns.description: Infrastructure patterns for human-in-the-loop agent execution — how to halt, persist state, notify, and resume.

real_time_sse.name: Server-Sent Events streaming
real_time_sse.use_case: user-facing applications — digital assistants, chat interfaces
flow: 1. user sends request → agent processes until requires_confirmation trigger
2. agent signals halt → immediately close SSE connection (do NOT hold connection open waiting for human)
3. persist full agent state to State Store (database)
5. user clicks → frontend hits dedicated Execute Endpoint (not the chat endpoint)
6. execute endpoint loads context by ID → runs pending action
real_time_sse.principle: Never hold an active connection open waiting for human input — persist state and close. Humans take coffee breaks; connections don't survive them.

async_event_driven.name: Asynchronous event-driven workflows
async_event_driven.use_case: background processing — invoice auditing, batch operations, no active user session
flow: 1. Celery worker executes agent logic until threshold requiring intervention
2. worker saves agent context and pending actions to database, then terminates
3. notification sent to approval surface (Slack message, email) with interactive buttons
4. user clicks button → hits callback API endpoint
5. callback endpoint loads serialized context by unique ID → executes approved action
async_event_driven.principle: The notification surface is also the trigger surface. Workers don't wait idle — they save state and exit. Supports hours or days of human response latency.

state_store.description: Database-backed persistence for halted agent executions. Enables thousands of concurrent pending approvals without degrading performance.
state_store.implementation: PostgreSQL table or JSON schema — keyed by unique execution ID
state_store.cleanup: TTL-based expiration for unclaimed halted states — prevent unbounded growth

metrics_additions: hitl-approval-latency: time between halt and human response
hitl-approval-rate: percentage of halted actions that are approved vs. denied
hitl-pending-count: number of currently halted executions awaiting approval

metrics: request-latency: p50, p95, p99 per endpoint
llm-cost: tokens consumed and dollar cost per call and per session
llm-latency: time to first token, total generation time
retrieval-quality: precision and recall per RAG query
task-queue-depth: Celery queue length and processing time
agent-budget: per-agent API cost tracking with hard caps
webhook-throughput: events received, processed, failed per provider

budget_control.description: Without strict controls, a single autonomous agent loop can easily exceed $50 in API costs.
mechanisms: max-turns: hard cap on agent reasoning iterations per session
per-session-budget: dollar limit per agent invocation
model-tiering: use cheaper models for classification/routing, expensive models only for final generation
alert-thresholds: notify when daily/weekly spend exceeds configured percentage of budget

trace_based_debugging.description: In non-deterministic AI systems, traditional unit tests are insufficient. Trace analysis is the primary debugging tool.
reveals: full conversation tree including system prompts, tool calls, and intermediate reasoning
whether the model is reasoning over outdated history
whether a tool output is providing contradictory information
whether a bloated prompt is causing instruction neglect at Turn 15+
where exactly in the context engineering the failure occurred
failure_hierarchy: 1. analyze the actual trace — see what the model 'saw' at the moment of failure
2. check context quality — too much noise? too little signal? outdated information?
3. identify failure source — RAG retrieval? tool description? conversation history? system prompt?
4. only then refine the prompt — use positive few-shot examples, not more prohibitions

principles: Every LLM call is traced end-to-end: input, output, latency, tokens, cost.
Structured logs (JSON) with correlation IDs across request lifecycle.
Alerts on error rate spikes, not just individual errors.
Health checks verify actual functionality (can query DB, can reach Redis), not just process uptime.
Persist a trace of every event in the database — logs and DB traces are the only way to debug an evolving AI system.
Monitor cost as aggressively as you monitor errors — an uncontrolled agent loop is a financial DoS.
When an agent fails, trace before you prompt-fix — context is often hidden during development but explodes in production.
Manual trace inspection has the highest value-to-prestige ratio in AI engineering — automation does not replace the need to look at the data.
Capture raw events (exact JSON flowing through the system) — every production failure isolated as a Level 1 unit test becomes a permanent regression guard.

raw_event_capture.description: When a failure is identified via Langfuse or user feedback, capture the exact data that caused it.
workflow: 1. identify failure in production traces or user reports
2. extract the raw JSON event that triggered the failure
3. isolate as a Level 1 unit test with the exact input and expected output
4. fix the system locally, verify the test passes
5. redeploy — the same failure mode never recurs
raw_event_capture.principle: Every production failure becomes a permanent regression test. The test suite grows from real-world failures, not hypothetical scenarios.

cloud_deployment.description: Production deployment patterns for AI applications — from MVP to scaled service.

infrastructure_as_code.principle: Infrastructure is versioned alongside code — environment definitions (render.yaml, docker-compose.yml, GitHub Actions) are committed to the repo.
tools: docker-compose: local dev and single-server production

instance_sizing.principle: Test on target cloud instance before committing to architecture — heavy ML models cause OOM on standard tiers.
patterns: start with the smallest viable instance — upgrade only when profiling proves the bottleneck
agent processing with concurrent LLM calls typically requires 2GB+ RAM
prefer lightweight tools (Rust-based parsers) over heavy Python ML models to stay on smaller instances

cron_scheduling.pattern: Celery Beat for recurring pipeline runs (daily digest, hourly sync)
cron_scheduling.alternative: platform-native cron (Render, Railway) for simpler schedules without Celery overhead

security_hardening: inbound-ip-restrictions: only authorized services can access the database backend
firewall: strictly limit traffic to ports 80 and 443 — all others closed by default
internal-networking: use private Docker networks for DB-to-API communication
default-deny: all traffic blocked by default — whitelist only necessary services
rotating-proxies: for scraping or external API access, use rotating residential proxies to prevent IP blocking (WebShare or equivalent)

scaling_path: Start: single VPS with Docker Compose (backend + frontend + postgres + redis)
Growth: separate database to managed PostgreSQL, add Celery workers
Scale: load balancer (Nginx) + multiple backend instances, CDN for frontend
Enterprise: container orchestration (ECS/Kubernetes), managed Redis, read replicas
